headline: Black Tuesday, the Lateran pacts, and the slow collapse of the postwar order
summary: 1929 broke the fragile prosperity of the postwar decade. The New York Stock Exchange crashed on Black Thursday (24 October) and Black Tuesday (29 October), erasing tens of billions of dollars of nominal wealth and beginning what would become the Great Depression. Herbert Hoover was inaugurated in Washington on 4 March; the Young Plan restructured German reparations and the Reichsbank president Hjalmar Schacht warned it would fail. In February Mussolini and Cardinal Gasparri signed the Lateran Treaty, creating Vatican City and reconciling the Italian state with the Holy See. In China Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government at Nanjing consolidated a fragile Northern Expedition. Palestine erupted in the Western Wall riots. Soviet Russia began Stalin's first Five-Year Plan and the forced collectivisation of agriculture. The year would echo violently through the 1930s.

snapshots.text: Europe entered 1929 in what looked like a hard-won prosperity. In Britain the second Labour government under Ramsay MacDonald took office on 5 June after a general election that returned no majority. In Weimar Germany the Grand Coalition under Chancellor Hermann Muller struggled to keep together Social Democrats and pro-business parties as the Young Plan, negotiated in Paris under the American banker Owen Young and signed in June, restructured German reparations at 121 billion gold marks over 58 years. The plan was ratified only after Hjalmar Schacht resigned from the Reichsbank in protest and Alfred Hugenberg's DNVP, allied with an ascendant Adolf Hitler and his NSDAP, campaigned against it in a referendum that failed in December. In Italy on 11 February Mussolini's foreign minister and Cardinal Pietro Gasparri, papal secretary of state, signed the Lateran Treaty at the Lateran Palace, resolving the Roman Question of 1870 and creating the sovereign Vatican City state. In the Soviet Union Stalin had by year's end expelled Trotsky (deported to Turkey in February) and turned on the right opposition of Bukharin, Rykov and Tomsky; the first Five-Year Plan of forced industrialisation was inaugurated and collectivisation of agriculture accelerated with the year of the great break in December.

The United States began 1929 with the inauguration of Herbert Hoover on 4 March, a Stanford-trained mining engineer promising a permanent end to poverty. The stock market climbed through the summer to a Dow Jones peak of 381.17 on 3 September. Then it broke. On Thursday 24 October (Black Thursday) volume reached 12.9 million shares and prices plunged before a consortium of bankers led by Thomas W. Lamont of J. P. Morgan steadied them; on Monday 28 October the Dow fell 13 percent; on Black Tuesday 29 October it fell another 12 percent on 16.4 million shares. By mid-November roughly 30 billion dollars of market value had been wiped out. The crash did not immediately cause the Great Depression, but it broke a credit chain that within three years would collapse a third of the American banking system. In Mexico the Cristero War between the government and Catholic guerrillas ended in June through an agreement brokered by US ambassador Dwight Morrow. In Argentina Hipolito Yrigoyen was serving his second Radical presidency amid growing military discontent. In Brazil Washington Luis's Cafe com Leite politics was heading for the 1930 Revolution.

China in 1929 was formally reunited under Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government at Nanjing, whose Northern Expedition had defeated the Beiyang warlords in 1928. The Kuomintang inaugurated what its official histories called the Nanjing Decade of tutelary rule under a party-state modelled on Sun Yat-sen's Three Principles of the People. The reality was fragile: 1929 saw the Chiang-Guangxi War in the spring and the beginning of the Central Plains War against Feng Yuxiang, Yan Xishan and Wang Jingwei that would peak in 1930. In the Jinggang mountains of Jiangxi, Mao Zedong and Zhu De's Fourth Red Army held their Gutian Conference in December, consolidating Communist Party control of the army. In Manchuria the young marshal Zhang Xueliang, having assassinated the pro-Japanese official Yang Yuting in January, moved against Soviet control of the Chinese Eastern Railway; a border war with the Soviet Union in the summer and autumn ended in a stinging Chinese defeat and the Khabarovsk Protocol. In Japan Prime Minister Hamaguchi Osachi's Minseito government took office in July committed to the gold standard and naval limitation. In colonial Korea, Governor-General Yamanashi Hanzo faced growing labour unrest and the student uprising at Gwangju in November.

British India in 1929 was working through the aftermath of the boycott of the all-white Simon Commission on constitutional reform. In December at the Lahore session of the Indian National Congress, presided over by the young Jawaharlal Nehru at his father Motilal's insistence, Congress adopted the resolution of Purna Swaraj, complete independence, on 31 December and unfurled the tricolour on the banks of the Ravi. The date of 26 January 1930 was chosen for the first Independence Day pledge; Mohandas Gandhi would launch the Salt March three months later. In April the young revolutionaries Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt threw non-lethal bombs in the Central Legislative Assembly to protest the Public Safety Bill; Bhagat Singh's subsequent hunger strike made him a national hero. Viceroy Lord Irwin issued the October Declaration promising eventual Dominion status. In Afghanistan a civil war of 1928-29 ended King Amanullah's reforms; the Tajik bandit-king Habibullah Kalakani briefly held Kabul before being toppled by Nadir Khan, who took the throne in October. In Persia Reza Shah Pahlavi, on the throne since 1925, was pushing hard modernisation, army reform and rail construction.

The Middle East in 1929 was governed by the mandatory system created at San Remo in 1920. In British Palestine long-simmering tension over Jewish and Muslim access to the Western Wall in Jerusalem exploded into the August Riots (Buraq Uprising) of 23-29 August, in which 133 Jews were killed, most notoriously in the Hebron massacre of 24 August, and 116 Palestinians killed, mostly by British police fire. The Shaw Commission of Inquiry reported in March 1930. In British Iraq King Faisal I ruled under a mandate that would end in 1932 with Iraq's admission to the League of Nations. In French Syria the ruinous Great Syrian Revolt of 1925-27 had been suppressed. In the Kingdom of the Hejaz and Nejd, Abdul Aziz ibn Saud was completing his conquest of the peninsula; the last of the Ikhwan revolts under Faisal al-Dawish was crushed at Sabilla in March 1929. In Egypt King Fuad had suspended the constitution in 1928; new elections in December 1929 returned the Wafd Party of Mustafa el-Nahhas Pasha. In the Maghreb the French protectorate over Morocco under Marshal Lyautey's successors continued the pacification wars in the Atlas, while Italian forces in Libya under Marshal Pietro Badoglio and General Rodolfo Graziani were escalating the brutal reconquest of Cyrenaica against Omar al-Mukhtar.

Colonial Africa in 1929 lived under the aftermath of the First World War and the Berlin Conference borders. In British Nigeria, Igbo and Ibibio women in Owerri and Calabar provinces launched the Aba Women's War from 18 November, protesting the imposition of taxation by the warrant-chief system; British troops and police killed at least 55 women before order was restored, and the incident forced a reorganisation of indirect rule in southeast Nigeria. In the Union of South Africa J. B. M. Hertzog's Pact government pushed through the Native Administration Act and continued the segregationist consolidation that would become apartheid. In colonial Kenya settler agitation and Kikuyu land loss deepened. In French West Africa under Governor-General Jules Carde the recruitment of Africans as tirailleurs and as forced labour on the Thies-Kayes railway continued. In Ethiopia the empress Zewditu died on 2 April; Ras Tafari Makonnen was crowned as Negus (king) later that year and would become Emperor Haile Selassie I in November 1930. In Liberia the Fernando Po labour scandal, in which government officials were accused of shipping forced labourers to Spanish plantations, prompted a League of Nations inquiry in 1930. Global commodity prices for cocoa, palm oil, cotton and coffee were about to collapse.

In Australia the Bruce-Page Nationalist-Country coalition government fell on the industrial front. Prime Minister Stanley Bruce, seeking to abolish federal arbitration in the Maritime Industries Bill, was defeated on the floor of the House on 10 September, and Labor under James Scullin won the ensuing election on 12 October. Scullin took office on 22 October, one day before the New York crash; the depression would define his government. In Western Australia commercial radio broadcasting expanded; in Canberra the new parliament house, opened in 1927, hosted a full year of debate. In New Zealand Prime Minister Joseph Ward's United (Liberal) government struggled with declining export prices for wool and dairy. In Papua New Guinea and the mandated territory of New Guinea Australian administrations were expanding into the highlands: the Leahy brothers' great highland patrols were still a year or two off, but Michael Leahy was preparing his first prospecting expedition. In the American-administered Philippines the Independence Missions of Manuel Quezon and Sergio Osmena lobbied Congress. Across the Pacific islands the copra economy was already contracting under the first tremors of the world depression.
